Through the research that has been presented, we can see that in Charlottesville there is a greater divide in economic disparity compared to similar counties. 
Due to this, there becomes a group of disenfranchised students who not only lack a consistent way to school, but also lack internet at home as well as lower SOL scores/literacy rates. 
On top of this fact, they may not be able to afford after school programs/tutoring that might help them catch up to the rest of their class.
While Charlottesville has one of the greater average spending per student for the counties that we looked at, they do not necessarily get the same attention as the students from other counties due to the larger class sizes in Charlottesville.
This causes them to fall behind their peers, and continue on to the next grade without the same general knowledge. Because of this, when they get to high school, the overall gap between these groups only widens, leaving the same group failing and struggling with their SOLs/literary exams without any help to give them. 
Due to these factors, we have lower scores/passing rates in lower income areas, moving on to different grades and graduating with their peers while being left behind in further stages of life. 
The best way we can help get rid of this crisis is to help the kids early in life by putting them in after-school programs, reallocating funding to help the disenfranchised such as allowing for more internet access, and reducing the staff student ratio.
